claims.text: Andrew Dawson, an ordinary 34-year-old TikTok user from British Columbia, became the center of one of the year's biggest internet mysteries after posting videos of an alleged giant on a Canadian mountain in April 2022.

evidence.quote:
strange turn and
Andrew Dawson would become the center of
one of the biggest internet mysteries in
years every story I cover on this
channel is a suggestion from you and
almost every one of them falls into

claims.rationale: The Andrew Dawson TikTok 'giant on the mountain' saga is a real online phenomenon documented across TikTok, Reddit, and the Confessionals podcast in mid-2022. It accumulated millions of views and substantial discussion. The 'biggest internet mysteries of the year' framing is the host's editorial gloss but the underlying viral phenomenon is real.

claims.text: On April 9, 2022, Andrew filmed what he believed was a giant humanoid figure on the ridge of a mountain in Alberta, Canada, that subsequently disappeared when he returned to look for it.

evidence.quote:

friend when he spotted something on the
ridge of whistler's peak in Alberta
Canada
what is it
person dude
no seriously pull over pull over
although his friend wasn't convinced
An

claims.rationale: Andrew did film and post the now-famous April 2022 'something on the mountain' video — that part is documented. Whether what he saw was actually unexplained is the contested point; in his own verdict, the host concludes it was the top of a cell phone tower on what is actually Canoe Mountain (not Whistler's Peak / not Alberta — the host corrects his own location attribution later in the verdict).

claims.text: The region around Whistler / Jasper has a deep history of Sasquatch sightings — over 200 reported since the 1800s — backed by Salish indigenous tradition that goes back roughly a thousand years.

evidence.quote:
mal do
you know how hard it is to keep it
steady with zoomed in all the way

Bigfoot or Sasquatch sightings going
back to the 1800s and native tribes in
this area have a legend about Sasquatch
that goes back a thousand years in fact

word for wild man or hairy man the last

claims.rationale: British Columbia and the Pacific Northwest are indeed cited as having the highest Sasquatch sighting density in North America, with the BFRO logging hundreds of reports across the region. The Salish-language origin of 'Sasquatch' (anglicized from sasq'ets) is well documented; coastal Salish nations have a long oral tradition of large hairy beings (sometimes called sásq'ets, t'simnit, etc.). The 'thousand years' figure for the oral tradition is plausible but not strictly documentable.

claims.text: When Andrew returned to investigate, he was stopped on a public road in Jasper National Park by a man he believed to be a CIA agent, who told him he was trespassing and ordered him to turn around.

evidence.quote:
s stopped by some type of
government agent
I just got stopped by like some CIA
agents he told me to turn around and
said I'm trespassing I said it's a
public freaking it's a public area like

claims.rationale: Andrew did post a video describing being stopped on the road by someone he characterized as a CIA agent. The CIA is the U.S. foreign intelligence service and has no operational mandate inside Canada, so the 'CIA' attribution is incorrect on its face; the host walks this back in his own verdict and notes the agent doesn't sound like trained law enforcement and was driving a Dodge Charger with aftermarket exhaust — neither typical of professional surveillance.

claims.text: Three weeks after going silent, Andrew posted an 'official update' video that fans found deeply suspicious — recorded on a different phone, from a tripod, with him glancing off-camera as if directed — claiming all his prior videos had been scripted entertainment.

evidence.quote:

the videos that I post all the videos
that I posted were scripted they're just
fake they were just strictly for an
entertainment
I sorry to disappoint you guys I just
need to give yo

claims.rationale: The 'official update' retraction video is a real, widely-discussed artifact in the Dawson saga, and the production differences (tripod, gaze direction, aspect ratio) are observable and were noted in contemporary online analysis.

claims.text: Ten days after the retraction, Andrew posted a follow-up retracting the retraction — captioning it 'too much has happened and I can't be forced to be silent' — claiming his original videos were real after all.

Andrew's life had bec

claims.rationale: The second retraction video and its caption are well-documented in coverage of the case (Confessionals podcast, Reddit threads, news write-ups). The interpretation of what it meant remains contested but the post itself is real.

dependencies.rationale: The second video explicitly negates the retraction in the first.

claims.text: Andrew Dawson was reported dead on July 1, 2022, at age 34 — about six weeks after his last TikTok video — with an obituary posted in the Campbell River Mirror that fueled online speculation that he had been silenced for what he saw on the mountain.

evidence.quote:
huh good idea nope he didn't go to
the medium what was in the paper Andrews
obituary oh no on July 1st about six
weeks after Andrew's final Tick Tock
video his obituary appeared in the
Campbell River mirror speculation online
went wild was his death a coincidence or
is this yet another example of a
whistleblower suddenly disappearing and
what was it that Andrew saw on the
mountain if anyone was looking for
closure on these Mysteries they weren't
going to get it Andrew Dawson passed
away on July 1st 2022

claims.rationale: An obituary for Andrew Dawson did appear in the Campbell River Mirror on July 1, 2022. As the host notes in the verdict, the paper allows obituary submissions without a death certificate, so the obituary alone is not proof. Multiple commenters and acquaintances have since confirmed his death (reportedly by suicide). 'Silenced for what he saw' is the conspiracy framing; the documented facts support that he died but not the cause-and-effect implication.

claims.text: On reflection, the 'giant' Andrew filmed was actually the top of a cell phone tower visible from one stretch of BC-5 / Yellowhead Highway near Cedarside Regional Park — and it disappeared in his follow-up video simply because the road curves west and the tower goes behind terrain.

evidence.quote:
see
this is the right mountain and if we
zoom into that Peak we can see the
object what Andrew thought looked like a
giant was just the top of a cell phone
tower but the reason the tower
disappeared in a later video is because
he was recording a little further down
the highway where the road veers to the
West that puts the tower out of you the
UFO video was taken over a different
mountain now while this could be a UFO
it's probably just contrails the reason
it looks bright or

claims.rationale: The host's geolocation is the most plausible explanation. The 'object' on the ridge in Andrew's footage matches the silhouette of cell antennas mounted on a peak, and the apparent disappearance from a slightly different vantage on the same highway is exactly what you'd expect when terrain occludes a small static object. This was the dominant debunking explanation in the post-mystery analysis.

dependencies.rationale: Identifies the 'giant' as a mundane cell tower.

claims.text: The other 'mysterious' phenomena in Andrew's videos are equally mundane: the UFO is sunrise contrails, the helicopters are heli-logging crews removing trees from inaccessible areas, and the 'overnight' black structure is the Jasper SkyTram, built in 1964.

evidence.quote:
probably just contrails the reason
it looks bright orange is because the
video was taken while the sun was coming
up now the helicopter video is fun lots
of stories we cover seem to have black
helicopters circling around but that's
not what's Happening Here what Andrew
saw is called hella logging this is when
helicopters are brought in to remove
trees from inaccessible areas and that
building that Andrew saw that was
actually the Jasper Sky tram even how
did

claims.rationale: Heli-logging is a real and common forestry practice in BC and Alberta; the Jasper SkyTram is a real aerial tramway opened in 1964 (Canada's longest and highest aerial tramway) and visible at a distance from the Yellowhead Highway. Sunrise contrails routinely appear orange. All three explanations match well-documented reality far better than UFO/black-helicopter/secret-base alternatives.

claims.text: The 'CIA agent' was almost certainly not law enforcement — the casual demeanor, the personal vehicle (a 2015 Dodge Charger with aftermarket exhaust), and parking conspicuously outside the target's house are textbook violations of professional surveillance tradecraft.

evidence.quote:
t's not them being a
jerk police are trained to establish
Authority at the beginning of an
encounter this guy doesn't sound like a
cop to me and definitely not like a Fed
yeah man just just turn around please
now the quote unquote agent was driving
a 2015 Dodge Charger we know that some
Canadian law

claims.rationale: The host's tradecraft analysis is sound. Real surveillance does not involve parking with engine running and headlights on outside the subject's home; that's the opposite of staying covert. The Dodge Charger Pursuit is a fleet vehicle for some Canadian agencies, but with aftermarket exhaust it doesn't read as a fleet car. The verbal manner ('yeah man just turn around please') is uncharacteristic of trained federal officers in either Canada or the US.

claims.text: Despite the host's thorough debunking, Andrew's death itself remains the hardest part of the story to dismiss as a hoax — the timing of the obituary (published 7am the same day he died), the difficulty of faking one's own death, and his sister's social-media silence about her brother's passing all leave residual unease.

claims.rationale: The host explicitly declines to render a verdict here, naming both possibilities (good hoax vs. tragic real death). directTruth omitted because the host himself does not commit and the meta-claim is intentionally uncertain — too unsettled to assign a number without overstepping.
